text: This presentation comes from Jim Hospital and Research Center, Coimbatore, India, and myself, P. Santillanathan, one of the co-authors.
The surgical team demonstrates the port placement for the colectomy, with two camera work trocars positioned at the suprapubic region and the umbilicus.

The umbilical port is positioned to access the left side and pelvis, while the suprapubic camera is utilized for visualizing the transverse colon and right side colon mobilization during the multi-quadrant surgery.
The surgeon begins pelvic dissection, similar to the anterior resection approach, identifying and dividing the inferior mesenteric pedicle.
The surgeon uses a medial approach to identify the inferior mesenteric artery by pulling the mesocolon. The view is from the right side, showing the bifurcation of the artery as dissection begins.

The surgeon applies a liga clip and uses a ligasure device to divide the tissue distally. The flap is raised, preserving retroperitoneal structures such as the ureter, and is separated from the Gerota's fascia, revealing the vascular embryonic plane.
The surgeon navigates the embryonic plane of fusion, aiming to minimize blood loss. The plane is visually challenging to identify in both thin and obese patients.

The surgeon incises the line of Toldt laterally, reaching up to the lateral abdominal wall. This incision is made along an avascular attachment, facilitating the separation of the colon from the lateral abdominal structures.
The surgeon dissects retrocholically, advancing towards the splenic flexure of the colon. The laparoscopic camera provides a continuous view of the dissection process up to the splenic flexure.
The surgeon divides the gastrocolic omentum, revealing the fully mobilized splenic flexure. The inferior border of the pancreas is visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon divides the entire inferior border of the pancreas from its attachment to the transverse colon and mesocolon. A small vessel, initially mistaken for the middle colic artery, is identified as a branch within the body of the pancreas, clipped, and divided.
The surgeon exposes the retroperitoneal structures, revealing the vessels within an avascular plane. Careful dissection is performed to avoid damaging the vessels.
The surgeon navigates to the pelvis, performing total mesorectal excision within the defined holy plane, ensuring careful dissection to avoid inadvertently entering a deeper plane.

The surgeon navigates through an avascular plane, carefully preserving visible vascular and nerve bundles, including sympathetic and parasympathetic nerves. The approach transitions from posterior to anterolateral, and finally anteriorly, revealing the pelvic diaphragm and the clearly visible brown levator ani muscle.
The surgeon prepares to mobilize the right colon, with the levator muscle visible outside the surgical field.
The surgeon incises the peritoneum below the cecum and proceeds to mobilize the hepatic flexure of the colon. The duodenum is identified, preserved, and gently pushed back to maintain its position.
The surgeon creates a retrocolic tunnel and approaches the vascular pedicle, identifying the middle colic and right colic vessels. The vessels are skeletonized at their origin and then divided.

The surgeon manipulates the hepatic flexure of the colon downward using a cartilaginous maneuver, exposing the duodenum and the head of the pancreas. This reveals the middle colic vessels, which are approached supracolic, clipped, and divided.
The surgeon completes the mobilization of the colon, ensuring clear visualization of the surgical field across multiple quadrants.
The surgeon uses an endocutter to divide the terminal ileum, freeing the specimen at one end. Retracting stitches are applied to maintain exposure and facilitate further dissection.
The surgeon performs an intrasphincteric dissection from below, dividing the internal sphincter. The entire specimen is then delivered out through a transanal approach.
The surgeon uses Lone Star retraction to assist in the removal of the entire colon, rectum, and a portion of the terminal ileum, completing the specimen extraction.
The surgeon creates an intracorporeal pouch using a laparoscopic approach, shaping the pouch within the abdominal cavity.

The surgeon uses two firings of a 60mm stapler to create a 12cm long pouch, which is then exteriorized and anastomosed to the external sphincter, completing a coloanal anastomosis, specifically an ileal pouch anal anastomosis.
The surgeon performs a covering ileostomy and places a drain, marking the completion of the colectomy procedure.
